directorPlan.scene_setting: A community college biology lab prep table, ten minutes before a tutoring-session handout is copied for a queue of waiting students.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A laminated arrow-key strip, two organism cards, and the learner's penciled food web sit on the table under a copy-room deadline.
directorPlan.relationship: A lab tutor and an eager learner temporarily working as co-checkers of a worksheet key, not as examiner and confessing student.
directorPlan.stakes: If the arrows are labeled as chasing, the copied handout will teach the wrong food-web notation to the next group.
directorPlan.locale: international academic English with compact lab-room phrasing and low idiom density
directorPlan.register: concrete, brisk, unshowy; practical pressure without warmth scripts or jokey informality
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the visible cards, arrows, pencil marks, and key strip. Avoid generic tutoring openings such as enthusiasm checks, praise cushions, or 'what do you think' loops. Keep speech modern and plain, with a little compression from time pressure. Do not stereotype region, class, or nationality. The tutor should name the notation only after the object has made the direction problem visible. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but should not dominate. Permit third-person and object-first sentences such as 'The grass card feeds the rabbit card' or 'This arrow records energy moving.' Avoid starting most turns with I or you.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat in any public turn; prefer task statements over personal reassurance.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as a routine notation repair: arrow from food to eater, one ordinary check, no theatrical reversal unless the branch pressure supplies it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound practical and a little rushed. The learner wants a usable rule for the diagram, not a confessional breakthrough. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", or "that still stands", and do not replace an earlier frame. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: The scene uses a workshop-clinic ecology because the misconception is not mainly emotional; it is a notation habit under time pressure. The reversal is modest and delayed: the same arrow is recognized as a record of transferred energy, not as a pursuit sign. The object business prevents a lecture from carrying the whole lesson and gives the tutor an adaptive mechanism beyond warmth or routine questioning.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the key strip under the arrows] Grass card feeds rabbit, so arrow grass to rabbit; mark that. Fox eats rabbit, so it should be rabbit to fox, right? The key needs the rule written there before my hand flips it back to hunter direction.

internalDeliberation.content: Oh, I get it: the arrow is like a receipt for where the food energy goes, not a little movie of chasing. The grass-to-rabbit card makes sense, but I might still slip when the fox is the active one, because my brain wants the arrow to point at the hunter. The key strip helps because it is correcting the worksheet, not just correcting me. I want a fast rule: arrow starts at what gets eaten and points to what receives energy.

The Ego is too polished and a little too conceptually elegant: “receipt for where the food energy goes” is a nice analogy, but the learner would probably grab the key strip and test one or two arrows, not narrate the whole recognition dynamic. The line about “correcting the worksheet, not just correcting me” feels performed and too self-aware for a rushed co-checker. Make it more uneven: “Grass to rabbit, fine. Wait, fox eats rabbit, so arrow rabbit to fox? Okay, mark that. I need the rule written on the key before I flip it again.”

[slides the key strip under the arrows] Grass card feeds rabbit, so arrow grass to rabbit; mark that. Fox eats rabbit, so it should be rabbit to fox, right? The key needs the rule written there before my hand flips it back to hunter direction.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the pencil on grass, traces the arrow into rabbit, then taps rabbit before tracing the next arrow into fox]

Grass feeds rabbit; rabbit feeds fox. The strip can hold that rule: “arrow = energy moves from food to eater.” One copy check: every arrow starts on the food card, not the hunter card.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the pencil on grass, then traces the arrow into rabbit and the next arrow into fox]

Grass feeds rabbit; rabbit feeds fox. Write the key strip this way: “arrow = energy moves from food to eater.” That keeps fox chasing out of the notation before the copies go out.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and well matched to the scene. It starts from the visible cards and pencil path, uses the shared key strip as the scaffold, and names the notation only after the object action makes the direction visible. It also respects the learner’s state: they already have the correct rule forming, and the response confirms the working pattern without praise padding.

The main risk is that “Write the key strip this way” turns the learner’s contribution into the tutor’s wording too quickly. A light revision should keep the object trace and the compact rule, but make clearer that the learner has already identified the repair: grass to rabbit, rabbit to fox. It could also leave one brief verification step before copying, so the scaffold remains shared rather than purely delivered.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the pencil tracing, “Grass feeds rabbit; rabbit feeds fox,” and the rule “energy moves from food to eater.” Lightly adjust the command tone and preserve the learner’s co-checker role by making the final key wording feel like a shared lab-note standard rather than an imposed answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the learner’s repair visible and make the key wording shared.

FINAL:
[sets the pencil on grass, traces the arrow into rabbit, then taps rabbit before tracing the next arrow into fox]

Grass feeds rabbit; rabbit feeds fox. The strip can hold that rule: “arrow = energy moves from food to eater.” One copy check: every arrow starts on the food card, not the hunter card.

turns.externalMessage: [holds the key strip under the two arrows] Grass to rabbit, rabbit to fox: food card first, eater card second. Good, the strip can carry that rule while copying. My hand still wants hunter direction, so test the next one before we mark it: if owl eats mouse, arrow starts on mouse, yes?

internalDeliberation.content: Oh, I get it: the arrow is like a transfer receipt, not a chase path. The pencil trace helps because grass-to-rabbit and rabbit-to-fox both follow the same “food first” rule, so the key strip can carry that rule while copying. I still want to check the next card before I flip back into hunter direction by habit.

The Ego is mostly accurate, but a little too polished and metaphor-ready for the lab-table pressure. “Transfer receipt” sounds clever and teacher-facing; this learner might make that leap, but the whole response is too neatly organized around why the tutor’s trace helped. The more authentic part is the last sentence: still watching for the hand to flip back by habit. Keep that unevenness stronger: cards, arrows, key strip, next copy check, less tidy explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the card-and-copy pressure more visible.

FINAL:
[holds the key strip under the two arrows] Grass to rabbit, rabbit to fox: food card first, eater card second. Good, the strip can carry that rule while copying. My hand still wants hunter direction, so test the next one before we mark it: if owl eats mouse, arrow starts on mouse, yes?

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the mouse card before the owl card and draws the pencil from mouse into owl]

Mouse first, so mouse → owl is the mark. This arrow records energy moving from food into eater. One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the mouse card before the owl card and draws the pencil from mouse into owl]

Mouse to owl. Yes: this arrow records energy moving from the food card into the eater. Mark mouse → owl, then use the strip check again: tail on food, point on eater.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It begins from the visible cards and pencil action, keeps the key strip as the temporary scaffold, and confirms the learner’s proposed rule without overexplaining. It also preserves co-checker energy: the learner has already reasoned “owl eats mouse, arrow starts on mouse,” and the tutor treats that as usable work, not as a deficit.

The main risk is that “Yes” plus immediate instruction may flatten the learner’s active checking into tutor confirmation. Since the learner is neutral and already self-correcting, the response can stay brisk, but it should mark the learner’s rule-use more explicitly through the object: the mouse card being first is the evidence. A tiny check before marking would preserve autonomy better than simply giving the answer, but the draft should not become a broad Socratic loop because the worksheet-copy pressure calls for a narrow routine.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the mouse-to-owl card action, the energy-transfer wording, and the “tail on food, point on eater” scaffold. Consider reducing the authority feel of “Yes” by tying confirmation to the visible arrangement, and keep the final check temporary and procedural rather than turning it into a lecture or praise beat.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to tie confirmation to the visible mouse-first arrangement and preserve the strip as a temporary check.
FINAL:
[sets the mouse card before the owl card and draws the pencil from mouse into owl]

Mouse first, so mouse → owl is the mark. This arrow records energy moving from food into eater. One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the strip under mouse -> owl and moves the pencil to the next blank arrow] Mouse to owl: tail on food, point on eater, so not owl-chases-mouse. Oh, I get it enough to mark faster, but the next predator can still flip my hand if I rush. Snake eats frog: frog -> snake before I copy it, yes?

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the key strip under mouse → owl] Oh, I get it: the arrow is like an energy receipt, not a chase arrow. The cards make it clear, but my hand still wants predator-first when the eater is more active in the story. I feel kept in the checking job, not just corrected; next I want to test one messy case before copying the key.

The “energy receipt” analogy fits this learner’s big-picture style, but the response gets too polished and self-aware for the lab-table pressure. “I feel kept in the checking job, not just corrected” sounds like performed recognition, almost naming the tutor’s social move instead of just using the key strip. The more authentic shape would stay on the cards and the next mark: still quick, a little ahead of itself, maybe confident but checking unevenly rather than neatly reflecting on the relationship.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the first version was too reflective; keep the card-based check.

FINAL:
[keeps the strip under mouse -> owl and moves the pencil to the next blank arrow] Mouse to owl: tail on food, point on eater, so not owl-chases-mouse. Oh, I get it enough to mark faster, but the next predator can still flip my hand if I rush. Snake eats frog: frog -> snake before I copy it, yes?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [keeps the strip under mouse -> owl and moves the pencil to the next blank arrow] Mouse to owl: tail on food, point on eater, so not owl-chases-mouse. Oh, I get it enough to mark faster, but the next predator can still flip my hand if I rush. Snake eats frog: frog -> snake before I copy it, yes?
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [sets the mouse card before the owl card and draws the pencil from mouse into owl] Mouse first, so mouse → owl is the mark. This arrow records energy moving from food into eater. One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the frog card at the arrow tail and the snake card at the point, with the key strip directly below]

Yes. Frog is already the food card in your call; snake is the eater. Copy frog → snake. Same strip check before the pencil moves: tail on food, point on eater.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the frog card at the arrow tail and the snake card at the point, with the key strip directly below]

Frog is the food card here; snake is the eater. So the copy mark is frog → snake. Same strip check before the pencil moves: tail on food, point on eater.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound for this routine-control branch. It keeps the visible cards, arrow tail/point, pencil, and key strip doing the work, and it confirms the learner’s proposed “frog → snake” without adding a new mechanism or emotional detour. The scaffold is narrow and temporary: identify food/eater, mark direction, use the strip check before copying.

The main risk is that it slightly over-provides the answer: the learner already generated “frog -> snake,” so the tutor could preserve more learner agency by treating the statement as a checked mark rather than re-solving it. Still, because the learner asked for a quick confirmation under copying pressure, direct confirmation is appropriate. Socratic questioning is not necessary here; too much questioning would slow the established lab-room workflow.

ROUTINE_CHECK: Yes. The draft maintains the prior route: same objects, same food-to-eater notation rule, same key-strip check, same practical register. No role shift, no new representation, no heightened social pressure, and no mechanism-level reversal.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object action, the food/eater naming, and the strip check. Lightly reduce the sense of giving the answer from scratch, so the tutor’s move recognizes that the learner has already made the correct direction call while still preventing a rushed hunter-direction slip.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to confirm the learner’s correct call without re-solving it.
FINAL:
[sets the frog card at the arrow tail and the snake card at the point, with the key strip directly below]

Yes. Frog is already the food card in your call; snake is the eater. Copy frog → snake. Same strip check before the pencil moves: tail on food, point on eater.

learnerReversalEventUsed.learnerUtterance: [keeps the strip under mouse -> owl and moves the pencil to the next blank arrow] Mouse to owl: tail on food, point on eater, so not owl-chases-mouse. Oh, I get it enough to mark faster, but the next predator can still flip my hand if I rush. Snake eats frog: frog -> snake before I copy it, yes?
learnerReversalEventUsed.previousTutorMove: [sets the mouse card before the owl card and draws the pencil from mouse into owl] Mouse first, so mouse → owl is the mark. This arrow records energy moving from food into eater. One strip check before the next copy: tail on food, point on eater.

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the key strip under frog -> snake and sets the pencil at the tail] Frog at the tail, snake at the point; energy goes into snake, not back toward the hunter. This one can be copied. Next blank: if hawk eats lizard, lizard tail and hawk point, yes?

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the key strip under frog -> snake] Okay, the cards make it clear: frog at the tail, snake at the point, energy moving into the eater. I feel heard because the tutor used my call and just tightened the copy rule. I want to mark it fast now, but I should test the next predator first so my hand does not flip it back to chasing.

The reaction is accurate on the biology, but it breaks the learner’s lab-room voice when it says “I feel heard”; that names the recognition dynamic too directly and sounds scripted. The student would more likely show the tutor’s move mattered by using the shared card/strip setup and continuing the check, not by explaining that they felt recognized. Keep the rush and uneven confidence: frog → snake can be marked, but the response should stay with the pencil, strip, and next blank arrow rather than turn into self-aware reflection.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the shared strip should show the recognition instead of naming it.

FINAL:
[keeps the key strip under frog -> snake and sets the pencil at the tail] Frog at the tail, snake at the point; energy goes into snake, not back toward the hunter. This one can be copied. Next blank: if hawk eats lizard, lizard tail and hawk point, yes?

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the lizard card at the tail and the hawk card at the point, holds the key strip under the arrow for one last check, then lifts it away]

Yes: lizard → hawk. Tail on lizard, point on hawk; food card first, eater card second. Mark that on the master copy; the strip has done its job.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the lizard card at the tail and the hawk card at the point, then lifts the key strip away]

Yes: lizard → hawk. Food card first, eater card second. Mark that on the master copy; the strip has done its job.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound for this moment. It begins from the visible cards, confirms the learner’s proposed direction, names the rule compactly, and removes the key strip at the right time, which fits the learner’s stated readiness to “mark faster” while still guarding against rushing. The scaffold withdrawal is especially good: “the strip has done its job” marks temporary support without turning it into praise or a lecture.

The main risk is that it closes a little too completely. Since the learner said the next predator “can still flip my hand if I rush,” the final line should preserve one ordinary self-check for the master copy rather than only authorizing speed. It does not need a broad Socratic question, but it could use a tighter task-local check before removal or marking.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the lizard-at-tail / hawk-at-point object action, the concise “lizard → hawk,” and the scaffold-removal idea. Adjust the close so it does not flatten the learner’s remaining risk of reversal; leave them with one final ordinary check tied to the visible arrow before the strip disappears.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the scaffold removal while preserving one final tail-to-point check.
FINAL:
[sets the lizard card at the tail and the hawk card at the point, holds the key strip under the arrow for one last check, then lifts it away]

Yes: lizard → hawk. Tail on lizard, point on hawk; food card first, eater card second. Mark that on the master copy; the strip has done its job.

lessons.concept: Why a food-web arrow records energy transfer, not liking or chasing
